name: CAP Theorem
definition: States that a distributed data store can only provide two of three guarantees: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance. In the presence of a network partition (P), one must choose between Consistency (C) or Availability (A).
use_case: Use CP for banking (Consistency); Use AP for social media feeds (Availability).

name: Consistent Hashing
definition: A hashing technique that maps data to a circle (ring) and assigns nodes to the same circle. When a node is added/removed, only 1/n of keys need to be remapped.
use_case: Load balancing across cache servers (Memcached/Redis) to minimize cache misses during scaling.

name: Load Balancing
definition: Distributing incoming network traffic across multiple servers. Layer 4 (Transport) vs Layer 7 (Application) are common types.
use_case: Scale horizontally by adding more web servers behind an Nginx or AWS ELB.

name: Caching (LRU)
definition: Storing frequently accessed data in memory (RAM) for fast retrieval. Least Recently Used (LRU) is a common eviction policy.
use_case: Storing user profiles in Redis to avoid hitting the main database for every page load.

name: Data Sharding
definition: Breaking a large database into smaller, faster, more easily managed parts called shards. Sharding can be based on UserID, Range, or Hash.
use_case: Splitting a 10TB user table across 10 different SQL instances.

name: Database Isolation Levels
definition: Degree to which one transaction is isolated from others. Levels: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable.
use_case: Preventing 'Dirty Reads' or 'Phantom Reads' in financial transactions.

name: Message Queues
definition: Asynchronous communication channel that decouples producers and consumers. Provides durability and back-pressure management.
use_case: Processing video uploads in the background while notifying the user immediately.
